title: Double-dip glaze on a two-colour bowl
subtitle: Dipping the base and rim of a bowl into two contrasting glazes
excerpt: A bisque-fired stoneware bowl is dipped base-first into one glaze and then rim-first into a contrasting glaze, leaving an overlapping band where the two glazes meet. The overlap zone produces a third effect where the glazes interact during firing.
sourceNotes: Double-dip two-colour glaze technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Ceramic work through a first low-temperature firing to harden it without glaze, leaving it porous and ready to accept glaze.
Immersing bisqueware in a bucket of liquid glaze to coat it in a single operation.
A wax emulsion applied to the foot ring and base to prevent glaze adhering there.
The band where two different glazes meet on a double-dipped piece. The two glazes flux together in the kiln, producing a third colour or texture distinct from either glaze alone.

content.text: The double-dip technique applies two different glazes to the same piece using two separate
dipping
operations. The interest lies in the
overlap zone

where the two glazes interact at firing temperature. Choose two mid-fire glazes that are compatible. Glazes from the same manufacturer's range are safest. A dark tenmoku and a cream shino, or a pale blue and a warm amber, both produce interesting overlap effects.

attrs.content: Use pre-mixed commercial glazes only. Wear an N95 mask throughout glaze sessions. Clean up wet — never dry sweep. All glaze containers and surfaces must be dedicated to ceramic use only.

items.name: Pre-mixed mid-fire glaze, colour A (for base)
Pre-mixed mid-fire glaze, colour B (for rim)

content.text: Preparing the bisqueware
Clean the
bisqueware
bowl with a damp sponge. Apply
wax resist
to the foot ring and the lower 10 mm of the base exterior. Allow the wax to dry completely before glazing.
First dip: base colour

Stir glaze A thoroughly. Grip the bowl at the rim with the tongs and dip it base-first into glaze A, submerging it to about two-thirds of the bowl's height. Hold for 3 seconds, lift, and allow to drain for 10 seconds. Set on the banding wheel and inspect. The base and lower two-thirds should be evenly coated; the upper third is bare bisqueware. Allow to dry fully, about 20-30 minutes.
Second dip: rim colour

Stir glaze B thoroughly. Grip the bowl at the base and dip it rim-first into glaze B, submerging it to about two-thirds of the bowl's height. This creates an overlap zone of 30-40 mm in the mid-section where both glazes are present. Hold for 3 seconds, lift, and drain. The interior of the bowl should receive the rim-dip coat on the upper portion; check this and touch up any gaps with the mop brush.
Touch-ups and loading
Use the mop brush to touch up the six tong marks from both dips. Check the waxed foot ring area is clean of glaze. Allow the piece to dry completely before loading the kiln.
Variations
Move the overlap zone higher or lower by adjusting the dip depth of each bucket. A narrow overlap band gives a sharper transition; a wide overlap gives a more diffuse blend.
Use the same technique on a cylinder or mug where the handle colour can visually tie the two glaze zones together.

items.symptom: Glaze peels off in the overlap zone before firing
items.cause: Second dip applied before the first coat was fully dry; wet glaze over wet glaze does not adhere
items.fix: Allow the first dip to dry until the surface is no longer cold or damp to the touch before dipping the second coat

items.symptom: Overlap zone looks muddy rather than interesting after firing
items.cause: Two glazes chemically similar; no visual contrast in the overlap
items.fix: Choose glazes with different surface qualities: a shiny and a matt, or a dark and a pale, for readable overlap effects
